title: Addressing Adversarial Attacks in IoT Using Deep Learning AI Models Paper Finding
contribution:
1. The proposed three‑stage filtering effectively cleans IoT data, removing adversarial noise while preserving important patterns.
  2. The Q‑COA optimized weighted adaptive batch normalization significantly boosts feature quality.
  3. The dual classifier (RCNN + RBM) with self‑attention weight‑sharing consistently outperforms all compared single models (RBM, RCNN, GRU, DCNN) on both datasets.
  4. Quantitative results:
    • AUC of 0.95 on dataset 1 (IoT/IIoT intrusion) and 0.97 on dataset 2 (RT‑IoT2022).
    • Accuracy, precision, recall, F1‑score all superior to baseline models (see Tables 3 & 4 in the paper).

The heterogeneous design (multiple models, adaptive weighting) provides defense diversity and redundancy, making it harder for an attacker to fool all components simultaneously.
dataset:
The authors used two publicly available datasets from Kaggle:

  1. IoT and IIoT Intrusion Dataset (referenced as [19]):
    • Link: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/munaalhawawreh/xiiotid-iiot-intrusion-dataset
    • Contains both normal and malicious network traffic from IoT and Industrial IoT.
  2. RT‑IoT2022 Dataset (referenced as [20]):
    • Available at UCI Machine Learning Repository: https://doi.org/10.24432/C5P338
    • A real‑time IoT dataset with diverse attack scenarios.

Both include sensor readings, device logs, network traffic, and cover consumer IoT as well as industrial settings.
detailed_notes_docx: uploads/1779267504_notes_A survey on adversarial attacks and defences.docx

title: A survey on adversarial attacks and deference.
contribution:
This paper categories deverbal attacks  into three categories.

1.      Evasion  Attack.

2.      Poisoning Attacks .

3.      Exploratory attacks.

4.      Black box attacks

5.      White box Attacks

6.      Targeted attacks

7.      Untargeted attacks
results:
Key Findings of the Paper

  1. DNNs are surprisingly fragile — tiny invisible changes to input can completely fool them, even when they have 99% accuracy on clean data.
  2. Szegedy et al. (2013) first discovered adversarial examples — this was the starting point of the whole field.
  3. White-box attacks are the strongest — full knowledge of the model means the attacker can craft near-perfect adversarial examples.
  4. Transferability is a major danger — one adversarial example can fool many different models, making black-box attacks very practical.
  5. No defense is complete — every proposed defense (adversarial training, distillation, feature squeezing, MagNet, etc.) can be bypassed under certain conditions.
  6. GAN-based attacks are powerful — attackers can use GANs to generate realistic fake data or steal information from collaborative learning systems.
  7. Poisoning attacks are harder to detect — they corrupt the training process itself, which is more dangerous and harder to fix than test-time attacks.
  8. JPEG compression surprisingly works well as a pre-processing defense across multiple attack types and settings (black-box, white-box, grey-box).
  9. NULL labeling (by Hosseini et al.) is described as the most effective defense at the time of writing.
  10. Defensive distillation was thought to be strong, but Carlini & Wagner broke it — showing that evaluating defenses is harder than creating them.
research_gap:
Gap 1: No Universal Defense Every defense works against some attacks but fails against others. i can research a combined/hybrid defense system that handles multiple attack types simultaneously.

Gap 2: Defenses for Black-box Attacks Adversarial training doesn't work well for black-box attacks. i can design defenses specifically for black-box scenarios.

Gap 3: Real-world Physical Attacks Most research is on digital images. Very little work on physical world attacks (e.g., printed adversarial stickers on real road signs). This is a hot research area right now.

Gap 4: NLP & Text-based Adversarial Attacks This paper mainly focuses on images. i can extend this to text models — fooling chatbots, sentiment analyzers, or spam filters with adversarial text.

title: A fuzzy-based frame transformation to mitigate the impact of adversarial attacks in deep learning-based real-time video surveillance systems
Viewpoint Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving AI
contribution:
The paper's main contribution is to explain and promote Federated Learning (FL) as a practical solution to two big problems in AI:

  1. Data Silos: Data is trapped inside different companies or departments and cannot be shared.
  2. Privacy Laws: New laws (like GDPR in Europe) make it illegal to collect and share people\'s data.

Term

Simple Definition

Example

Federated Learning (FL)

A way to train an AI model across many devices or companies that hold local data, without exchanging the data itself.

Training a keyboard app to predict your next word without sending what you type to a Google server.

Data Silo

Data that is trapped inside one department or company and cannot be easily shared with others.

A bank has customer data. An insurance company has different customer data. Laws prevent them from sharing. Each is a \"silo.\"

Horizontal FL (HFL)

Different parties have the same types of data (features) but on different people (samples).

Google has your typing data on your phone. Google has my typing data on my phone. The features (words typed) are the same, but the users (samples) are different.

Vertical FL (VFL)

Different parties have different types of data (features) on the same people (samples).

A bank (has your income/transaction features) and an e-commerce site (has your shopping history features) both have data on you (the same customer). They combine features to build a better loan risk model.

Federated Transfer Learning (FTL)

Used when parties have little overlap in both people (samples) and data types (features).

A hospital in the US (data on adult patients) and a clinic in Japan (data on elderly patients with different health metrics) want to collaborate. They have no common patients and different data. Transfer learning helps find patterns.

Differential Privacy (DP)

Adding \"random noise\" (a little bit of fake data) to the results so that you cannot tell if a specific person\'s data was used to train the model.

Before sharing the average height of 100 people, you add a random number like +0.2 or -0.3. The average is still useful, but you can\'t figure out Tom\'s exact height from it.

Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)

A way for multiple parties to jointly compute a result (like an average) using their private data, without anyone seeing the others\' data.

Alice knows a, Bob knows b. They want to know a+b without telling each other their number. They use a secure method. Bob sends a masked number to Alice, she adds her number, sends back, Bob removes the mask. They get the sum.

Homomorphic Encryption

A special type of encryption where you can do math on the encrypted data, and when you decrypt, the result is correct.

You have a locked box (encrypted data). I can add 1 to the number inside the box without ever opening it. When you open it, the number is increased by 1.

Private Set Intersection

A way for two parties to find the common users they both have data on, without revealing their full user lists to each other.

The bank and e-commerce site want to find their shared customers. They use a protocol to find the common IDs without the bank seeing the e-commerce\'s full customer list or vice-versa.

The key idea it contributes is: Instead of moving the data to a central computer to train an AI, we move the AI model to the data. Then, we only share the model updates (the \"learnings\") securely, without sharing the raw data.

Simple Example (The Study Group Analogy):

  • Old way (Centralized): You and your friends each have different chapters of a textbook. You all must give your books to one person who studies them all and makes a master guide. (You lose your books/privacy).
  • New way (Federated): You each study your own chapter at home. Then, you meet and only share your notes (model updates) with each other, without showing the actual book. You then combine the notes to create a master guide. Your private book never leaves your home.
results: it is a  review paper  and does not use any dataset
research_gap:
The paper explicitly lists these as \"practical challenges\" (see the Outlook section). These are perfect research gaps for a student or researcher.

  1. Slow & Unstable Communication: FL requires sending model updates over the internet. What if the connection is slow or keeps dropping?
    • Gap: Design new algorithms that are \"communication-efficient\" - they need fewer rounds of communication or can handle dropped connections without failing.
  2. Managing Many Devices (Scale): Google might want to use millions of phones for FL. How does a central server coordinate with millions of devices that can come and go?
    • Gap: Create new system architectures or aggregation algorithms that work reliably at a massive scale (e.g., 1 billion devices).
    •  
  3. Non-Identical Data (Non-IID): Your phone\'s data is not like my phone\'s data. I might type in English, you type in Hindi. I take photos of cats, you take photos of cars. This can make the global model perform poorly.
    • Gap: Develop new FL algorithms that work well even when everyone\'s local data is very different (this is a huge and active research area).
  4. Unbalanced Data: Some users have 10,000 photos, others have only 2. The model might become \"biased\" towards the users with more data.
    • Gap: Create fair aggregation methods that give smaller data owners a \"fair\" influence on the final model.
  5. Lack of Incentives: Why would I let my phone battery drain to help Google train its model? Why would a company share its model updates for free?
    • Gap: Design an \"incentive mechanism\" (like paying people in cryptocurrency or providing better service) to reward participants for joining the FL network.
dataset: it is a review paper does not have any dataset used

title: Federated learning for medical image analysis: A survey
contribution:
• Collects and organizes 77+ papers published between 2017 and 2023.
  • Introduces a new way to classify federated learning methods (by system parts: client, server, communication) — previous surveys didn't do this.
  • Lists and describes major software tools (PySyft, OpenFL, etc.) and datasets
  • Runs an actual experiment comparing three popular FL algorithms on Alzheimer's disease data
  • Discusses what problems still remain unsolved and what future researchers should work on.
results:
Can AI spot Alzheimer's from brain scans without sharing patient data?

            Researchers tested 6 ways to teach a computer to tell if a person has Alzheimer’s disease—just by looking at brain scans. 
            They used scans from two hospitals: Hospital A (428 patients) and Hospital B (360 patients). 
            The big question: Which method gives the best result while keeping patient information 100% private?
       
   

How Each Method Scored


Method Hospital A Score Hospital B Score
Cross (Worst) 81.8% 81.1%
Single 84.4% 82.8%
Mix (Illegal) 87.0% 87.2%
FedAvg 86.0% 84.2%
FedSGD 82.3% 84.4%
FedProx 85.8% 85.6%

 

What Each Method Does 

        Cross — The worst method: Teach the computer using only Hospital A’s scans, then test it on Hospital B without any changes. The scans from the two hospitals look different (like a selfie vs. a professional photo). The computer gets confused and makes many mistakes.
       
💡 Real life example: Learning to ride a bicycle, then being asked to ride a motorcycle without any practice.

   
        Single — Each hospital works alone: Hospital A trains and tests only on its own scans. Hospital B does the same. No sharing. Better than Cross because the test scans match the training scans, but each hospital has few patients, so the AI is not very smart.
       Real life example: Two teachers who never talk to each other — each only sees their own students’ work.
   

 


        Mix — Best but illegal: Put all scans from both hospitals into one big pile and train the computer on everything. This gives the best result (~87% correct). But sharing real patient scans is against privacy laws (like HIPAA). Cannot be used in real hospitals.
       Real life example: Taking every student’s answer sheet to make a perfect answer key — great result, but breaks all privacy rules.
   

 


        FedAvg — Best legal method (no data sharing): Each hospital trains its own AI locally — the scans never leave the hospital. Then they only share the “recipe” (a set of numbers, called weights) with a central computer. The central computer averages all the recipes and sends back a better one.
       
Real life example: Chefs cooking at home. They share only their recipes (not the actual food). A head chef combines them into one master recipe.

   

 


        FedSGD — A weaker version: Similar to FedAvg, but instead of sharing the final recipe, hospitals share tiny, messy step‑by‑step changes. Combining many noisy steps is harder and gives worse results.
       
Real life example: Sharing every pencil stroke you made while solving a math problem, instead of just the final answer.
   

 


        FedProx — Like FedAvg, but with a safety rule: Same as FedAvg, but it adds a simple rule: “Don’t stray too far from the group recipe.” This stops one hospital with unusual data from pulling the shared AI in the wrong direction.
       
Real life example: Same chefs, but now they are told: “Keep your recipe close to the group template.” One overconfident chef can’t ruin the whole thing.

   

 

The 4 Main Takeaways

     
  • Mixing all data gives the best score, but it’s illegal. You cannot move patient scans between hospitals. This method only shows the maximum possible result.
  • Using the same AI on a different hospital’s scans without any changes is the worst idea. Scanners are different, so the AI fails badly.
  • FedAvg and FedProx get almost the same high score as Mix, but without sharing data. Only 1% less accuracy, but 100% privacy. That’s the big win.
 
  • Sharing final recipes (weights) works much better than step-by-step notes (gradients). The final recipe is stable; the tiny steps are noisy.

   


       


            In short: wecan say thatb Hospitals can work together to build a very good AI for Alzheimer’s detection—as if they had pooled all their scans—without ever letting a single patient image leave the building. The small drop in accuracy (only about 1%) is a tiny price for keeping everything private.
research_gap:
New ideas i could explore

A few ideas inspired by this paper that could form a novel contribution:

Idea 1 — Federated learning for eye disease in low-resource countries. 

Idea 2 — Combining FL with large language models for radiology reports. The paper focuses only on images. What if the federated model also learned from radiology text reports alongside the images? Multi-modal FL with text + image is nearly untouched.

Idea 3 — Federated continual learning. Hospitals keep acquiring new images. How do you update an FL model over time without "forgetting" what it learned before? Combining FL + continual learning for medical imaging is wide open.  like real time systems

 

Gap

Explanation

Client shift handling

Current methods to reduce differences between hospitals’ data are not perfect. i can try new domain adaptation or personalized FL methods.

Multi-modality FL

Most FL works use only one type of image (e.g., MRI). i can work on combining MRI, CT, and PET in one FL system. in this survey paper the researcher olny works with the Alzheimer's disease data , so this is the main problem

Unseen clients (domain generalization)

How to make the FL model work well on a completely new hospital that was not part of training? Very little work exists.

Attack and defense in FL

Most FL systems are not secure. i can work on detecting or preventing poisoning attacks. i can also work on Adversial attack with this domain 

Medical video FL

Almost no work on FL for surgical videos or ultrasound videos. i can also work on the video survalance system or real time threat detecting system 

Model interpretability in FL

Doctors don’t trust black-box AI. You can work on explaining FL model decisions. . XAI can solve this problem i can work with the XAI also

  2. What tools/frameworks exist

title: An overview of implementing security and privacy in federated learning
contribution:
·         Most comprehensive survey of FL security and privacy attac as of 2024

·         Three-way analysis: model structure + attacker capability + model operation

·         Clear taxonomy — organizes attacks into security (Byzantine/poisoning) and privacy (member/feature/property inference)

·         Comparison tables (Table 2 and Table 3) — which defenses work for which attacks and at what cost

·         Literature analysis using CiteSpace — shows global research trends

·         Guidance for practitioners — tells you which defense to use for which attack type
results:
1.      FL is NOT inherently secure — even without sharing data, attackers can learn private information from model updates

2.      Security and privacy are separate concerns:

o    Security = protecting model integrity (Byzantine and poisoning attacks)

o    Privacy = protecting data confidentiality (member/feature/property inference)

3.      Byzantine attacks are most mature — well-studied with good defenses (Krum, Coordinate Median)

4.      Poisoning attacks are very hard to defend — especially backdoor attacks with small percentages (only 5% toxic samples can embed a backdoor!)

5.      Existing defenses always have trade-offs:

o    Differential privacy → less accurate model

o    SMPC → very high computation cost

o    Clustering defenses → need high-quality validation datasets

6.      Non-IID data makes everything harder — most defenses (including Krum) perform significantly worse when data is non-IID

7.      Malicious servers are more dangerous than malicious clients — a compromised server can see ALL updates and can isolate specific clients

8.      GAN-based attacks are increasingly powerful — traditional defenses struggle against GAN-generated poisoned samples

9.      Single attacker can still succeed — even a single malicious client with limited resources can complete model poisoning attacks (Bhagoji et al.)

10.  China and USA lead FL security research — but many gaps remain, especially in cross-device and vertical FL scenarios
research_gap:
The Problem Identified:

·         Existing defenses (Krum, Coordinate Median, FLDetector) work well for IID data but fail for non-IID data

·         Differential privacy protects privacy but reduces accuracy

·         No single defense works against ALL attack types simultaneously

The New Idea:
Build a multi-layer adaptive defense system that:

1.      Layer 1 — Reputation Scoring: Each client gets a reputation score based on history (like a credit score). First-time clients start with medium trust; proven clients get higher trust weights in aggregation

2.      Layer 2 — Non-IID-Aware Clustering: Instead of treating all updates equally, cluster them based on DATA DISTRIBUTION similarity (not just update values). Clients with similar data patterns should have similar updates; outliers are flagged

3.      Layer 3 — Adaptive Differential Privacy: Apply stronger noise to low-reputation clients and weaker noise to high-reputation clients. This maintains accuracy for honest clients while protecting against attackers

4.      Layer 4 — Encrypted Verification: For any update that passes Layer 1-3 but is still suspicious, request a zero-knowledge proof from the client to verify their data distribution without revealing actual data

Why This is Novel:

·         Current papers address non-IID OR reputation OR differential privacy separately

·         Nobody has combined all four in one system

·         This paper's gap analysis (Sections 5.4 and 6.4.4) clearly shows no defense handles all cases

·         The paper specifically says "there is always a trade-off between privacy and performance, and no single privacy security defence strategy can provide complete defence against all types of attacks"
dataset: CIFAR-10 with non-IID distribution (Dirichlet distribution), MNIST, CIFAR-100

title: Adversarial attack and defense technologies in natural language processing: A survey
contribution:
Three Main Contributions:

1.    New Classification System: They created a two-level classification for text attacks:

o    Level 1: Based on WHAT is changed (character, word, sentence, or multiple levels)

o    Level 2: Based on HOW it's changed (gradient-based, optimization-based, etc.)

2.    Comprehensive Defense Review: They organized defense strategies into:

o    Passive defenses (checking input)

o    Active defenses (training models to be stronger)

3.    Application Summary: They showed how attacks work on different NLP tasks (translation, question-answering, etc.)

title: Adversarial Attacks on Deep-learning Models in Natural Language Processing: A Survey
results:
• Section 2: Background Concepts

    • DNN: A function that maps input (X) to output (Y) using learned parameters (θ).
    • Perturbation: A small noise/change added to input to fool the model.
    • Adversarial Example: The "tricked" version of the input after adding perturbation.
    • Threat Model — describes the attack setup:
      • White-box attack: Attacker knows everything about the model (architecture, weights, etc.)
      • Black-box attack: Attacker only sees inputs/outputs, not the internal model.
      • Targeted attack: Attacker wants a specific wrong output.
      • Untargeted attack: Attacker just wants any wrong output.
      • Granularity: Whether the attack happens at character, word, or sentence level.
    • Also covers different deep learning architectures used in NLP: Feed-forward nets, CNNs, RNNs/LSTMs, Seq2Seq, Attention models, Reinforcement Learning, GANs/VAEs — explaining briefly what each does and how each has (or hasn't) been attacked.

    Section 3: From Image to Text

    • Reviews classic image-attack methods that inspired text attacks:
      • L-BFGS (Szegedy et al.) — first method to create adversarial images.
      • FGSM (Fast Gradient Sign Method) — a faster, simpler way (most copied method).
      • JSMA — uses sensitivity maps to find which input pixels/words matter most.
      • C&W Attack — optimizes attacks against defence systems.
      • DeepFool — finds minimal change needed to fool a model.
      • Substitute/Black-box attacks — train a copycat model to attack without needing full access.
      • GAN-based attacks — use generative models to create more natural-looking fake inputs.
    • Explains 3 key differences between attacking images vs. text:

    1.                Images = continuous values; Text = discrete symbols (words/letters).

    2.                Image changes are invisible to humans; text changes are usually obvious.

    3.                Small image changes don't alter meaning; small text changes can flip meaning entirely (e.g., removing "not" reverses a sentence's meaning).

    • Describes how text gets converted into numbers for DNNs:
      • Bag-of-Words / TF-IDF: Counts word frequency.
      • One-hot encoding: Each word/character gets a unique binary vector.
      • Dense encoding (word embeddings): Words become meaningful number vectors (e.g., Word2Vec) where similar words have similar vectors.
    • Lists ways to measure how big a text perturbation is (so it stays "small enough" to be valid):
      • Norm-based (mathematical distance)
      • Grammar/syntax checkers, perplexity (how "natural" a sentence sounds)
      • Semantic similarity (cosine similarity, Euclidean distance between word vectors)
      • Edit distance (Levenshtein distance, Word Mover's Distance)
      • Jaccard similarity (overlap between word sets)

    Section 4: The Core of the Survey — Attack Methods

    This is the heart of the paper. It organizes 40 papers into categories:

    White-box attacks (attacker knows the model):

    • FGSM-based (e.g., TextFool — inserts/modifies/removes "hot" words that matter most)
    • JSMA-based (uses sensitivity maps on text)
    • C&W-based (optimization attacks, e.g., Seq2Sick for sequence models)
    • Direction-based (e.g., HotFlip — flips characters using calculus tricks)
    • Attention-based (exploits attention weights to know which words matter)
    • Reprogramming (repurposes a trained model to do a different task without changing it)
    • Hybrid (combining image-attack methods adapted for text)

    Black-box attacks (attacker only sees input/output):

    • Concatenation Adversaries (e.g., adding a fake distracting sentence to a paragraph)
    • Edit Adversaries (typos, synonym swaps, character swaps — e.g., DeepWordBug)
    • Paraphrase-based Adversaries (rewriting a sentence differently, same meaning, fools the model)
    • GAN-based Adversaries (using GANs to create natural-looking fake text)
    • Substitution (training a copycat model)
    • Reprogramming (black-box version)

    Multi-modal/Cross-modal attacks (text + image/speech together):

    • OCR (Optical Character Recognition) attacks
    • Scene Text Recognition attacks
    • Image Captioning attacks
    • Visual Question Answering attacks
    • Speech-to-Text attacks

    Section 5: Defense Strategies

    • Adversarial Training: Train the model on both normal AND adversarial examples so it learns to resist them.
    • Data Augmentation: Add adversarial examples to training data.
    • Model Regularization: Add a mathematical penalty during training that punishes the model for being fooled easily.
    • Robust Optimization: Frame training as a min-max game — model tries to minimize error while a virtual "attacker" tries to maximize it.
    • Distillation: Train a second, "smoothed" model using the soft probability outputs of the first model, making it less sensitive to small changes.
research_gap:
as per this paper thje author included the future  work as mentioned below 

  • Perceivability: It's hard to make text attacks both effective AND invisible to humans.
  • Transferability: Attacks built for one model don't always work well on other models.
  • Automation: Many attacks still require manual work; full automation is still a challenge.
  • New Architectures: GANs, VAEs, and Differentiable Neural Computers are barely studied for attacks.
  • Iterative vs. One-off attacks: Iterative attacks (slow, very effective) vs. one-off attacks (fast, less effective) — there's a trade-off.
dataset: it is a suravy paper hence  it does not use any dataset

title: Why Should Adversarial Perturbations be Imperceptible? Rethink the Research Paradigm in Adversarial NLP
results:
Finding 1: Academic Attacks Don't Work in Reality

  • Methods that work well on sentiment analysis fail on security tasks
  • Attack success rates drop significantly (from 90%+ to ~50%)
  • Implication: Security tasks are fundamentally different

Finding 2: Simple Attacks Are More Effective

  • Character-level perturbations (like ROCKET) are more successful than complex word substitutions
  • Higher attack success rate (~75% vs ~50%)
  • Much more efficient (fewer queries)
  • Implication: Real-world attackers prefer simple methods

Finding 3: Word Substitution Destroys Meaning

  • Complex attacks (BERT-Attack) score poorly on validity (0.35/2.0)
  • Simple attacks (DeepWordBug) score highly (1.45/2.0)
  • ROCKET scores best (1.98/2.0)
  • Implication: Academic methods often change the meaning so much that the attack fails

Finding 4: Current Defenses Are Inadequate

  • SOTA defenses detect 70-90% of academic attacks
  • Same defenses detect only 15-35% of ROCKET attacks
  • Implication: Real-world defenses need to be redesigned

Finding 5: Task Difficulty Varies

  • Misinformation and spam are hardest to attack
  • Toxic content is relatively easy to attack
  • Implication: Different security tasks need different approaches

Finding 6: Efficiency Matters

  • ROCKET requires very few queries (often <50)
  • Academic methods require hundreds of queries
  • Implication: Real-world attacks need to be fast

Finding 7: The Research Gap is Real

  • There's a significant disconnect between academic and real-world adversarial research
  • Implication: The community needs to change its approach

Datasets Used

All combined into their released collection Advbench (available on GitHub: thunlp/Advbench), each with both balanced and unbalanced versions.

  • They collected 10 real-world datasets across 5 actual security tasks:
    1. Misinformation (fake news) — datasets: LUN, SATNews
    2. Disinformation (fake reviews) — datasets: Amazon-LB, CGFake
    3. Toxic content (hate speech) — datasets: HSOL, Jigsaw2018
    4. Spam — datasets: Enron, SpamAssassin
    5. Sensitive information leakage — datasets: EDENCE, FAS
  • They cleaned each dataset (removed duplicates, fixed imbalance, etc.) and released both "balanced" and "unbalanced" versions.

title: A Survey of Attacks on Large Language Models
contribution:
Major Contributions:

1.    Comprehensive Taxonomy (Classification System)

o    First paper to organize attacks into THREE lifecycle phases

o    Further subdivides attacks by strategy (input-based, weight-based, etc.)

2.    Unified Framework

o    Bridges the gap between separate attack types

o    Shows how different attacks relate to each other

3.    Agent-Specific Attack Analysis

o    Extends beyond single LLMs to LLM-based agents

o    Highlights unique vulnerabilities in multi-agent systems

4.    Defense Evaluation

o    Summarizes existing defenses for each attack

o    Identifies which defenses work and which don't

5.    Research Gap Identification

o    Points out where current defenses fail

o    Proposes future research directions

6.    Benchmark Reference

o    Lists representative attacks in tables

o    Provides a starting point for researchers
results:
Major Findings:

1.    LLM Security is a Lifecycle Problem

o    Attacks can happen at ANY stage (training, inference, deployment)

o    Defending only one stage is not enough

2.    Backdoor Attacks are Sophisticated

o    Attackers have 4 methods: input-based, weight-based, reasoning-based, agent-based

o    Modern backdoors are hard to detect (stealthy, composite triggers)

3.    Jailbreaking is Evolving

o    Moved from manual prompts to automated generation

o    New methods use reasoning, psychology, and low-resource languages

o    Once jailbroken, AI can jailbreak other AIs

4.    Prompt Injection is a Fundamental Issue

o    LLMs can't distinguish between instructions and data

o    This is a design flaw, not a bug that can be easily fixed

5.    DoS Attacks are Resource-Based

o    Attackers can force AI to generate endless text

o    This costs money and degrades service quality

6.    Watermarking is Fragile

o    Many attacks can remove or bypass watermarks

o    Need more robust watermarking methods

7.    Agent Systems Multiply Risks

o    More components = more attack surfaces

o    Attacks can spread between agents

8.    Current Defenses are Insufficient

o    Most defenses target specific attacks

o    Adaptive attacks evade existing defenses
research_gap:
Gap 1: Agent-Specific Security

Problem: Multi-agent systems have unique vulnerabilities
What i Can Implement:

·         Study how attacks spread between agents in a system

·         Develop communication protocols that detect infected agents

Gap 2: Real-Time Adaptive Defense

Problem: Defenses can't adapt to new attacks
What i Can Implement:

·         Build a system that learns from past attacks

·         Use machine learning to predict new attack patterns

Gap 3: Explainable Defense

Problem: When defenses block something, we don't know why
What i Can Implement:

·         Create a defense that explains why it flagged an input

·         Build visualization tools for attack detection

Gap 4: Watermarking Robustness

Problem: Watermarks can be easily removed
What i Can Implement:

·         Design watermarks that survive paraphrasing

·         Create detection methods that work even if text is modified

Gap 5: Low-Resource Language Safety

Problem: LLMs are less safe in rare languages
What i Can Implement:

·         Expand safety training to more languages

·         Create multilingual safety datasets

title: A Survey of Adversarial Attacks: An Open Issue for Deep Learning Sentiment Analysis Models
contribution:
1. First comprehensive review specifically focused on adversarial attacks in sentiment analysis
    • Previous reviews covered all text applications
    • This one focuses only on sentiment analysis
  2. New taxonomy for classifying attacks in sentiment analysis
    • Organized attacks by: model access, analysis level, granularity, element selection, strategy, DNN model
  3. Self-contained framework
    • Includes all basic concepts needed to understand adversarial attacks
    • Doesn't require reading other papers first
  4. Identifies research gaps
    • Points out what hasn't been studied yet
    • Suggests future research directions
  5. Summarizes defenses
    • Lists and explains current defense methods
    • Shows which defenses work against which attacks
results:
1. Deep Learning Models Are vulnerable
    • All tested sentiment analysis AIs can be tricked
    • Even state-of-the-art models like BERT aren't safe
    • Success rates of attacks are high (often >80%)
  2. Different Attacks Work Differently
    • Character-level attacks (misspellings) are easy to do
    • Word-level attacks (synonyms) are harder to detect
    • Sentence-level attacks (paraphrasing) are most natural
  3. Black Box Attacks Are Still Effective
    • Even without knowing the AI, attacks can work
    • This is dangerous because attackers don't need inside information
    • Example: Someone can attack your AI without ever seeing its code
  4. Defenses Help But Aren't Perfect
    • Adversarial training improves robustness
    • But models can still be tricked with new attacks
    • No defense is 100% effective
  5. Research is Growing
    • Number of papers on this topic has increased rapidly
    • More researchers are studying both attacks and defenses
    • The field is still young and developing
  6. Challenges Remain
    • Making attacks imperceptible is hard
    • Transferability is limited
research_gap:
Gap 1: Perceptibility - Making Changes Invisible

  • Current Problem: Text changes are easy to see
  • Possible Research: Use synonyms that are less common but still correct
    • Instead of "happy" → "h@ppy" (visible)
    • Try "happy" → "joyful" (more natural)
    • But "joyful" might not trick the AI as well

Research Question: Can we find words that humans think are synonyms but AI thinks are different?

 

Gap 2: Transferability - Making Attacks Work Everywhere

  • Current Problem: Attack on one AI might not work on another
  • Possible Research: Find universal changes that fool all AIs
    • If we know what all AIs are bad at, we can attack those weaknesses

Research Question: What patterns make ALL sentiment AIs confused?

 

 

 

Gap 3: Attack-Independent Defenses

  • Current Problem: Defenses know about the attack method
  • Possible Research: Create defense that works for any attack
    • Like a general "security check" for all text
    • Check grammar, spelling, and meaning

Research Question: Can we create a defense that just checks if text is "natural" regardless of attack type?

 

 

Gap 4: Real-World Testing

  • Current Problem: Most attacks tested on clean datasets
  • Possible Research: Test attacks on real-world messy data
    • Social media has misspellings anyway
    • Harder to distinguish attacks from real mistakes

Research Question: Can we detect attacks when data is already messy?
dataset:
The paper reviews studies that used these datasets:

 

 

Note: The paper itself doesn't use datasets (it's a review paper). These are datasets used by the papers being reviewed
